meaning: A condition where the speed of a computer system is limited by the time it takes to perform input/output operations.
example: The database query is I/O bound because it has to read millions of rows from the hard drive.
example2: Upgrading the SSD fixed the I/O bound performance issues of the server.
example3: A program that downloads many small files is typically I/O bound.
Explanation: I/O bound (Input/Output bound) occurs when the time taken to complete a task is determined mainly by the speed of the storage or network rather than the speed of the processor (CPU). For example, if a program is waiting for a slow hard drive to load data or a slow internet connection to download a file, the CPU stays idle. In this state, buying a faster CPU won't help; you need faster disks or better network bandwidth. This is very common in web servers and database management systems. Identifying if a system is I/O bound is a primary step in performance tuning and IT troubleshooting.
commonContext: Software performance, system administration, and hardware optimization
usageTip: Use 'I/O bound' to explain why a computer is slow despite having a fast processor.
commonMistake: Thinking a computer is always 'slow' because of the CPU; often it is actually I/O bound.

meaning: A system or process limited by the speed of input/output operations
example: The application is I/O bound because of the slow database.
example2: I/O bound tasks spend more time waiting than computing.
example3: Upgrade the disk if the process is I/O bound.
Explanation: An 'I/O bound' process is one where performance is limited by the time taken to read or write data, rather than by the CPU's processing speed. This is a crucial concept for performance tuning and choosing appropriate hardware. It is an adjective. If a task is I/O bound, adding more CPU power will not improve speed; instead, faster disks or networks are required. It is very common in server logs.
usageTip: Use when identifying why a system is slow.
commonMistake: Trying to solve I/O bound problems with better CPUs.
